# Agentic Flow v2.3.0 - run log

**Date:** 2026-04-03
**Branch:** `v2.3.0`
**Scope:** Logging template standardization and run observability improvements
**Compared to:** `v2.2.0`

---

## Version Updates

### Change Summary

Use this section to describe what changed in this version compared to the previous one.
Prefer concrete, implementation-focused entries.

| Area | How it was | What is changed | What is now |
|------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Log template / observability | Run logs evolved organically across versions and had no single standard structure for comparing changes, findings, costs, and outcomes | Added a reusable `.github/agentic-flow/log-template.md` and used it as the baseline for new logs | Run logs now have a unified structure with explicit version updates, user observations, agent analysis, and cost/context tracking to improve observability |
| Code styling / consistency | Code formatting depended on manual habits, and several quality expectations from `documentation/code-guidance.md` still relied mainly on reviewer judgment instead of repo-enforced tooling | Added Spotless with `palantir-java-format` for `flow-orchestrator`, introduced a user-friendly `scripts/final-check.sh` wrapper for the final flow, aligned `mcp-server` formatting checks with Prettier, tightened Checkstyle naming/finality coverage, re-enabled SpotBugs defensive-copy enforcement, and removed the now-tool-enforced local-variable-finality rule from `code-guidance.md` | Agents and users now have a single final-check command for formatting plus verification, stronger automatic enforcement of code-quality and consistency rules, less reviewer-only interpretation for objective checks, and a cleaner guidance document focused on rules that still need human judgment |
| Project structure / naming conventions | Project structure and naming patterns had already improved in code, but the documentation still described them only partially and did not clearly define the naming suffixes used across layers | Updated `documentation/code-guidance.md` to document root package responsibilities, capability-root class placement, and naming conventions for `Service`, `Port`, `Adapter`, `Mapper`, `Properties`, `Request`, `Response`, `Dto`, and `Input`; aligned the high-level structure note in `.github/copilot-instructions.md` | The documented architecture now matches the current codebase more closely, which should reduce ambiguity in agent decisions and make package placement and naming more predictable |
| Agentic-flow review agent | Flow-level review relied mainly on the generic orchestration prompt and existing reviewer agents, which did not give a dedicated reviewer a fixed starting context for comparing the current run with the previous iteration | Added `.github/agents/flow-reviewer.agent.md` as a dedicated GitHub Copilot custom agent based on `.github/prompts/agentic-orchestration.prompt.md`; defined required context files, log selection rules, evidence-checking scope, and review output structure; set the model to `Claude Opus 4.6` | The flow can now be reviewed with a purpose-built Opus reviewer that starts from the right files, checks the latest and previous run logs explicitly, and produces more consistent, evidence-based agentic-flow analysis |
| Coder responsibility split (P1-A) | Coder agent had 12+ responsibilities per invocation (implement, test, verify, start app, curl, update smoke-test.sh, write verification log, update implementation report, parse Maven output). This caused terminal mega-commands, stalls, and missed handoffs across 4 consecutive versions | Stripped Coder to 5 core duties: implement, test, `verify-quick.sh` per slice, `final-check.sh` before handoff, return status. Moved app startup, smoke tests, verification log writing, and implementation report evidence to Team Lead. Moved smoke-test.sh design to Architect. Updated `java-coder.agent.md`, `team-lead.agent.md`, `java-architect.agent.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, and `agentic-flow-overview.md` | Coder now has a focused prompt that should reduce context rot, eliminate mega-commands, and produce faster handoffs. TL consolidates all evidence-gathering. Architect owns smoke-test design since it has the API and payload context |
| Reviewer code quality + triple verification (P1-B, P2-A) | Reviewer Phase 2 was an expensive terminal runner: re-ran `verify-quick.sh`, `final-check.sh`, startup, and smoke tests without performing meaningful code review. In v2.2.0 it missed every code quality issue the user caught manually. Three agents ran overlapping verification commands, wasting ~6-8% of context per feature | Rewrote Reviewer Phase 2: replaced mandatory script re-execution with evidence validation (re-run only if TL evidence is suspect); added explicit Code Quality Review checklist (naming consistency, model count, duplicate patterns, over-engineering, log context, test quality) referencing `code-guidance.md`; removed startup and smoke-test re-execution entirely. Updated `reviewer.agent.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, and `agentic-flow-overview.md` | Reviewer now focuses on code quality judgment that tools cannot provide, validates TL evidence instead of duplicating it, and uses less context per run. Each verification command is owned by exactly one agent |
| Agent model updates for copilot-cli | Models were set for VS Code IDE names only, preventing copilot-cli usage. PM was on Sonnet 4.5, Architect was on Sonnet 4.6 | Updated all 5 agent files to copilot-cli model names (`chatgpt`, `claude-sonnet-4.6`, `claude-opus-4.6`, `codex-5.3`, `gemini-3.1-pro`); kept IDE names as inline comments for easy switching. Upgraded PM from Sonnet 4.5 → 4.6 and Architect from Sonnet 4.6 → Opus 4.6 | Agents can now run in copilot-cli. Architect gets Opus-level reasoning for more complex plans. IDE names preserved for quick switch-back |
| Context map for targeted exploration | Architect and Coder scanned the full codebase to discover structure, wasting context on files unrelated to their task. As capabilities grow this becomes a major context-rot driver | Created `documentation/context-map.md` listing capabilities, packages, classes, endpoints, config, tests, and design notes. Wired into Architect and Coder preparation steps (read context-map first, then only target files). Added TL duty to update the map after every feature delivery. Added context-map as first Key Reference in `copilot-instructions.md` | Agents locate target code in ~50 lines instead of scanning 100+ files. Scales as capabilities are added. Human-readable too |
| Code refactoring (pre third-run) | `GitLabIssuesAdapter` contained repeated project-path resolution, optional query-param null checks, duplicated try/catch blocks, unnecessary `requireNonNull` wrappers, and class-level `@SuppressWarnings("null")` in tests | Extracted `GitLabUriFactory` for shared URI building, added `addOptionalParam(...)` helper, centralized `executeGitLabOperation(...)` for exception handling, removed unnecessary wrappers and test suppressions, improved create/delete logging context. Full report: `artifacts/refactoring/v2.3.0-refactor.report.md` | Adapter code is cleaner, DRY, and easier to extend for new capabilities. Tests are 77 green, all quality gates pass. This was a standalone refactoring unrelated to the second run |
| Smoke testing tooling (pre third-run) | Smoke tests relied on a brittle `scripts/smoke-test.sh` bash script using `curl` with manual status-code parsing, field-presence grep checks, and inline JSON payloads. Agents struggled with curl quoting, error handling, and maintaining the script as APIs evolved | Introduced Karate 2.0 as the structured API smoke-test framework. Added `src/test/karate/` test root with `.feature` files for health, search, create, delete, and lifecycle scenarios. Created `IssuesKarateTest` runner, `karate-config.js` with env-aware `baseUrl`, isolated Karate via Maven `-Pkarate` profile (excluded from surefire + default failsafe), and added `scripts/karate-test.sh` wrapper. Deprecated `scripts/smoke-test.sh`. Updated all agent files, `agentic-flow-overview.md`, `local-quality-rules.instructions.md`, `test-instructions.instructions.md`, and `context-map.md` to promote Karate as the default smoke-test tool and define agent responsibilities: Architect writes `.feature` files, TL executes against running app, Coder does NOT write or run Karate tests | Smoke tests are now structured, readable, assertion-rich Karate scenarios instead of fragile bash/curl scripts. Karate is fully isolated from unit and quality-gate runs. The Architect-writes-tests / TL-executes split keeps Coder focused on production code and unit/integration/component tests only |
| Agent prompt refinement (pre third-run) | Agent prompts had accumulated redundant instructions, inconsistent naming, and stale references across versions. TL prompt was 161 lines with duplicated context sections. Coder had overlapping must-not rules. Architect was blocked from writing Karate tests despite owning smoke-test design. Reviewer lacked handoff metadata and had vague rerun criteria. PM filename was inconsistent with other agents. Code-review-refactorer still referenced deprecated `smoke-test.sh` | Compressed TL prompt from 161 to 132 lines by merging context sections and tightening coder recheck and Phase 2 brief. Merged duplicate Coder must-not rules. Updated Architect to explicitly allow Karate `.feature` file writing. Added Reviewer handoffs frontmatter and defined concrete "suspect evidence" heuristic. Renamed `product-manager.md` to `product-manager.agent.md` and updated links. Fixed code-review-refactorer `smoke-test.sh` references to `karate-test.sh`. Updated Coder model to Sonnet 4.6 for third run | Leaner agent prompts with less redundancy, clearer Karate ownership, consistent file naming, and more precise Reviewer rerun criteria — all aimed at reducing context waste and improving agent focus in the next run |
| Delivery flow instructions consolidation (pre third-run) | Delivery-flow-specific rules were scattered across 3 sources: `blocker-escalation.instructions.md` (blocker rules, `applyTo: **`), `evidence-rules.instructions.md` (verification evidence, `applyTo: **/flow-orchestrator/**`), and the "Shared Workflow Rules" section in `copilot-instructions.md` (workflow control). None had `description` frontmatter to help the model decide relevance | Merged all three into a single `delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` with `applyTo: **`, a clear `description` targeting delivery flow agents, and three subsections: Workflow Control, Blocker Escalation, Evidence And Verification. Deleted the two old files. Removed the Shared Workflow Rules section from `copilot-instructions.md`. Added `description` fields to all remaining instruction files (`secret-redaction`, `local-quality-rules`, `test-instructions`) | One canonical instruction file for all delivery flow cross-agent rules. Cleaner `copilot-instructions.md` focused on project-level context. All instruction files now have descriptions for better model relevance hints |
| Local quality rules refactoring (pre third-run) | `local-quality-rules.instructions.md` was 100 lines with duplicated command descriptions (scripts described 3 times across Preferred Commands, Toolchain, and Review Guidance sections), verbose formatting and PMD/SpotBugs guidance sections, and agent-specific workflow rules that overlapped with agent prompts | Refactored to ~45 lines: merged all command descriptions into a single table, consolidated evidence recording rules, kept report outputs and quality config paths. Removed duplicate Review Guidance, Formatting Expectations, PMD Ruleset Guidance, and Coverage Gate sections. Added verification reference in `copilot-instructions.md` linking to the instruction file. Simplified Coder `Must` section to reference the instruction file instead of repeating script names. Simplified code-review-refactorer verification section to reference the instruction | Halved the instruction file size. Universal verification baseline in `copilot-instructions.md` ensures all agents follow the scripts. Agent prompts reference the instruction instead of duplicating script commands |
| Architect slice upper bound (post third-run) | Architect slice sizing principle from F-2 only had a lower bound ("resist many slices") with no upper bound. Run 3 Architect produced 1 monolithic slice with ~20 files, causing 4+ coder retries and ~1 hour of wasted time | Added upper-bound rule to Architect slice rules: a single slice touching more than ~8-10 files (production + test combined) should be split even if the work is mechanical | Architect now has both floor and ceiling for slice sizing, preventing overcorrection in either direction |
| Reviewer model change (post third-run) | Reviewer was on Codex 5.3 which consistently struggled with structured evidence validation and defaulted to terminal exploration, git commands, and redundant check re-runs across runs 2 and 3 | Changed Reviewer model from `GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)` to `Claude Sonnet 4.6 (copilot)` in `reviewer.agent.md` | Reviewer model is now consistent with Coder (Sonnet 4.6), which has shown better structured artifact writing and less terminal-obsessive behavior |
| Reviewer Phase 1 constitution crosscheck (post third-run) | Reviewer Phase 1 did not explicitly cross-reference story constraints against `constitution.md`. In run 3, PM wrote a mapping constraint that contradicted constitution Principles 2 and 8, and Phase 1 passed it through to the plan unchallenged | Added three new Phase 1 checks: (1) cross-reference every story constraint against constitution and blocker on conflict, (2) verify plan prose↔class-table consistency, (3) validate structural correctness of Architect-written Karate `.feature` files (syntax and assertion forms, no execution) | Phase 1 now catches story↔constitution conflicts, internal plan contradictions, and invalid test artifacts before coding starts — preventing the exact failure chain that caused the run 3 architectural issue and the Karate red card |
| Global git ban for all flow agents (post third-run) | Only TL had an explicit "Never run git commands" rule. In run 3, Reviewer (Codex) ran git commands despite this being prohibited in the delivery flow spirit | Added `[CRITICAL]` git ban covering all flow agents to `delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` § Workflow Control. TL retains its local reinforcement | Version control is now explicitly blocked for every agent in the flow, not just TL |
| PM constitution awareness (post third-run) | PM had no instruction to read `constitution.md` before writing locked constraints. In run 3, PM wrote a mapping-boundary constraint ("mapping must happen in orchestration") that directly contradicted constitution Principles 2 and 8 | Added PM instruction to read `constitution.md` before writing constraints and blocker on conflict | PM can no longer write locked constraints that contradict the architecture without being instructed to escalate |
| Constitution composition rule (post third-run) | `constitution.md` had no explicit rule governing whether integration adapters may internally call multiple external endpoints. Run 3 confusion originated from unclear boundary between "who composes multi-source data" | Added [MUST] rule under Principle 2: orchestration service coordinates multi-source calls; an integration adapter maps one external call to one orchestration contract | Clear ownership boundary for composition, prevents story constraints from directing multi-call composition into integration adapters |
| IssueDetail → EnrichedIssueDetail refactoring (post third-run) | `IssueDetail` contained a `changeSets` field (always `List.of()`) set in the integration mapper, violating the new composition rule. The integration layer was forced to compose a placeholder for data that will come from a different external source in Story 2 | Removed `changeSets` from `IssueDetail` (10 fields). Created `EnrichedIssueDetail(IssueDetail, List<Object> changeSets)` in orchestration layer. Updated `IssuesService`, `IssuesResponseMapper`, `IssuesController`, `GitLabIssueDetailMapper`, and all corresponding tests | Integration adapter returns pure single-source data; composition happens in orchestration. Story 2 (label events) will populate `changeSets` via a second port call composed in `IssuesService` |
| Architect scanning discipline (post third-run) | Architect spent ~20 minutes reading dozens of unrelated files (other features' plans, reports, reviews, unrelated source files) before writing the plan. This wasted ~33% of the Architect's runtime and contributed to context rot | Tightened Architect "Must not" rules: no reading other features' implementation artifacts; no exploring source files outside packages identified in `context-map.md`. Rewrote execution protocol with 8 prescriptive steps: checkpoint → story → context-map → constitution/code-guidance → targeted source files → verify external APIs → choose structure → write plan | Architect has a strict navigation funnel: checkpoint → context-map → relevant files only. Should eliminate wasteful exploration and reduce planning time from ~20 min to ~5-8 min |
| Context-map update ownership (post third-run) | `context-map.md` was maintained by TL at sign-off. Reviewer Phase 2 caught stale entries and red-carded the Coder, causing an extra round-trip. TL verification happened too late in the pipeline to prevent wasted time | Made Coder responsible for updating `context-map.md` during implementation (added to pre-handoff checklist step 5). Added context-map freshness to Reviewer Phase 2 code quality checklist. TL sign-off gate unchanged (still verifies) | Context-map is now updated by the agent that creates/changes files, validated by the Reviewer, and verified by TL — catching staleness early instead of at sign-off |
| Context-map zero-scan rewrite (post third-run) | `context-map.md` listed class names by package but not full file paths. Agents still needed `list_dir` / `file_search` to locate files, which cascaded into scanning unrelated directories. Missing entries: `GitLabUriFactory`, component test support classes, Karate feature files, individual test files, `FlowOrchestratorApplication`, config YAML files. Maintenance rule still said "Team Lead" after ownership was moved to Coder | Rewrote context-map as a file-level lookup table: every production class, test file, Karate feature, config file, and script has its own row with relative path and role. Added sections for application entry point, configuration files, and test infrastructure. Fixed maintenance rule to show three-tier ownership (Coder updates → Reviewer validates → TL verifies). Added `GitLabUriFactory` to shared infra and key patterns | Agents can now go directly from context-map to `read_file` without any directory exploration. No `list_dir` or `file_search` needed for known capabilities. Should eliminate the root cause of Architect scanning |

### Addressed Findings From Previous Log Analysis                                       

List how the issues and suggestions from the previous log were handled in this version.
If one issue required multiple actions, list them in the same cell as separate bullets.

| Issue spotted | How was addressed | What pain point should be solved |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| Gap 1 — Coder is the bottleneck (12+ responsibilities, mega-commands, stalls) | - Removed app startup, curl/smoke checks, smoke-test.sh updates, verification log writing, implementation report evidence from Coder<br>- Simplified evidence gathering to `PASS`/`FAIL` reporting<br>- Simplified handoff to 5-field summary<br>- Moved evidence-gathering duties to Team Lead<br>- Moved smoke-test design to Architect | Fewer coder loops, no mega-commands, less context pressure, faster handoffs |
| Gap 4 — Excessive TL↔Coder ping-pong from triple verification | - Replaced TL `mvn test` with `scripts/final-check.sh` as single recheck<br>- TL now runs final-check once per batch (not overlapping with Coder)<br>- Reviewer no longer re-runs startup or smoke tests; validates TL evidence instead | Clearer ownership: each verification command owned by exactly one agent |
| Gap 5 — Coder terminal mega-commands | - Replaced structured log-writing instruction with simple `PASS`/`FAIL` reporting<br>- Removed verification log section header requirement from Coder | No incentive to build bash parsers; evidence writing is TL's job |
| Gap 3 — Reviewer adds no code quality value | - Added Code Quality Review checklist to Reviewer Phase 2: naming consistency, model count, duplicate patterns, over-engineering, log context, test quality<br>- Replaced mandatory script re-execution with evidence validation<br>- Reviewer re-runs `final-check.sh` only when TL evidence is suspect | Reviewer focuses on judgment-based code review instead of being an expensive terminal runner |
| Gap 10 — No formatting tool | Added Spotless with `palantir-java-format`, `scripts/format-code.sh`, `scripts/final-check.sh` | Formatting issues eliminated permanently |
| Gap 6 — No package structure reference | Added Package Structure And Model Gate to `code-guidance.md` | Architect and Coder follow documented structure |
| Gap 8 — Command naming pattern | Added `*Input` suffix convention, renamed `CreateIssueCommand` → `CreateIssueInput` | No misleading CQRS implications |
| Run observability gap | Created `log-template.md` as reusable run log baseline | Structured, comparable analysis across versions |
| Reviewer flow-level analysis gap | Added `flow-reviewer.agent.md` dedicated agent | Purpose-built reviewer for system-level analysis |

## Run Notes (manually populated by user)

### First small feature run in CLI

- Started with copilot-cli at 15:40 - it actually affected the whole flow, needed to be re-checked with the second run in IDE as all models were picked up wrongly!! Architect was Sonnet (we already tried this model for the run) (but let's see, maybe smth obvious to fix will come out of this run)
- Issues - wrong model was picked up for team lead right ahead (sonnet)
- Looks like all models were decided by team lead, not an agent files - wrong names???
- Story was confrmed from 1 attempt (hoiku worked)
- Architect's plan confirmed from the first attempt (Sonnet worked, but still not opus)
- Architect plan had 6!!! slices for 1 field in 2 APIs update - that looks too extensive for such functionality, we need clear guidance on slice size otherwise it's loosing requests without obvious reasons
- Review phase 1 passed
- Context at this moment 27%, 10mins run
- First batch is returned to TL after Coder has implemented it (Coder is also Sonnet)
- TL ran checks and confirmed, then moced to coder with batch 2.
- 31% context used, 20 mins run
- Coder finished batch 2 cleanly, no red card, TL accepted the batch and moved to the last one
- Checked the plan - it looks too explicit for such small change - looks like model produces the same amount of lines no matter if it needs to implement the whole app or update logging... We need to check how to make it more reasonable - otherwise it's the whole for waisinting resources - we needed 1 batch for such small story, but got 3 batches.
- Coder passed last batch to TL, TL does the checks - all verifications passed
- Context 34%, 25 mins run
- TL passed to Reviewer for Phase 2 checks
- Reviewer returned back to TL as passed
- TL runs spot checks - do we now actually need than as TL just before reviewer ran final ones?
- TL confirms that it's ok and starting to work on sign-off
- TL created sign-off and started to commit the changes!!! Why? We don't need it - re-check Team Lead instructions - that's dangerous
- Signoff completed - context used 45%, time - 38mins. Both metrics are too high for such small feature. The main question is - workflow in such set up is not suitable for small stories or it's an issue of current setup. We should retest on normally sized feature and check what happens with the metrics.

- I would suggest to have 1 more run for normally sized feture, inside IDE, assign models as before, but for Lead I'd rather suggest Opus to test a theory that gpt can't invoke Opus because of different cost (as Opus works directly when passed in agent, but never as sub-agent).

## Mid-Work Analysis: Issues From First Run And Second Run Plan

**Analyzed by:** GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)
**Analysis date:** 2026-04-06
**Purpose:** Trace user-highlighted issues to root causes, plan concrete fixes, and define a second-run approach for a normally sized feature inside IDE.

### Issues Identified In First Run

| # | Issue | Evidence from run notes | Root cause | Severity |
|---|-------|------------------------|------------|----------|
| I-1 | Wrong model pickup — all agents ran Sonnet instead of configured models | "wrong model was picked up for team lead right ahead (sonnet)", "all models were decided by team lead, not agent files" | `copilot-cli` model name resolution differs from IDE; the TL `chatgpt` model name likely falls back to a default or the CLI resolves all sub-agent calls through the TL's own model tier | HIGH — invalidates the entire run as a model comparison test |
| I-2 | Excessive plan — 6 slices for adding 1 field to 2 existing APIs | "Architect plan had 6!!! slices for 1 field in 2 APIs update", "plan - it looks too explicit for such small change" | Architect prompt says "small, ordered, executable slices" without any proportionality guidance. The model fills the template structure regardless of feature size. No slice-count heuristic exists | HIGH — directly caused 3 batches, 3x TL↔Coder handoffs, and 45% context usage |
| I-3 | Too many batches — 3 coder batches for a trivial feature | "we needed 1 batch for such small story, but got 3 batches" | TL is hard-coded to max 2 slices per coder invocation. With 6 slices, that's 3 mandatory batches. No dynamic batch sizing exists | MEDIUM — consequence of I-2, but batch rule also needs flexibility |
| I-4 | TL spot checks after Reviewer are redundant | "TL runs spot checks - do we now actually need than as TL just before reviewer ran final ones?" | TL instructions mandate spot checks after Reviewer Phase 2, but TL already ran `final-check.sh` + startup + smoke tests before Reviewer. The post-Reviewer spot checks duplicate terminal work | LOW — small cost, but signals unclear ownership |
| I-5 | TL attempted to commit changes | "TL created sign-off and started to commit the changes!!! Why? We don't need it" | TL agent has no explicit prohibition on git operations. The line "Do not rely on git commits in this flow" is a soft hint about batch boundaries, not a ban on committing. The model inferred commit as part of sign-off completion | **CRITICAL** — destructive action, must be blocked |
| I-6 | 45% context / 38 min for tiny feature | Metrics in Post Run Checks | Compound effect of I-1 (wrong models), I-2 (6 slices), I-3 (3 batches), plus verification overhead at every batch boundary | HIGH — makes the workflow unsuitable for small stories at current settings |

### Planned Fixes For Second Run

#### Fix F-1: Prevent TL from running git commands (addresses I-5) — APPLIED

**Priority:** CRITICAL
**File:** `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md`
**Change:** Added to `## Boundaries` section:
```
- **[CRITICAL]** Never run git commands (commit, push, add, branch, checkout, reset). The user manages version control outside this workflow.
```

#### Fix F-2: Add slice sizing principle to Architect (addresses I-2) — APPLIED

**Priority:** HIGH
**File:** `.github/agents/java-architect.agent.md`
**Change:** Added a principle-based constraint after the 200-line cap. Instead of a rule-based file-count threshold, the instruction teaches the model *why* slices exist and when fewer slices are correct:
```
- **Slice sizing principle:** Slices exist to isolate implementation risk and provide meaningful
  verification checkpoints — not to decompose a plan into the maximum number of steps. Before
  defining slices, assess the overall scope: how many layers are affected, how much new logic is
  introduced, and how much of the change is mechanical (adding a field, wiring an existing pattern)
  versus genuinely new behavior. Mechanical changes that follow an established codebase pattern
  should be grouped together, not split into separate slices. A slice must represent a unit of work
  where something new can go wrong and needs independent verification. If the entire feature is a
  straightforward extension of existing patterns, one slice is the correct answer. Resist the urge
  to produce a longer plan when a shorter one is sufficient.
```
**Expected impact:** Architect produces plans proportional to actual complexity, not template length. Works for any feature type.

#### Fix F-3: Dynamic batch sizing for TL (addresses I-3) — DROPPED

**Reason:** Dropped per user decision. Large slices can confuse Coder when batched together. The 2-slice-per-batch cap stays as a safety net. The real fix is F-2 — fewer slices means fewer batches naturally.

#### Fix F-4: Clarify post-Reviewer spot checks are read-only (addresses I-4) — APPLIED

**Priority:** LOW
**File:** `.github/agents/team-lead.agent.md`
**Change:** Added to "Reviewer Audit And Red Card" section:
```
- Spot checks are read-only: review code, artifacts, and Reviewer reasoning. Do NOT re-run terminal
  verification commands (final-check.sh, verify-quick.sh, startup, smoke tests) that were already
  executed before Reviewer Phase 2.
```
**Expected impact:** Eliminates 1 redundant `final-check.sh` execution per run.

#### Fix F-5: IDE run with explicit model assignment (addresses I-1) — RUN CONFIG

**Priority:** HIGH — run configuration, no file changes
**Action for second run:**
- Run inside VS Code IDE, not copilot-cli
- Assign TL model to `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` to test the theory that `chatgpt` can't properly invoke Opus sub-agents
- Keep other agent models as currently configured (Architect: Opus, Coder: Codex, PM: Haiku, Reviewer: Gemini)
- Monitor which models actually execute at each stage

### Second Run Configuration

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Environment | VS Code IDE | CLI had model resolution issues |
| Feature size | Normal (new API endpoint or multi-layer change) | Small feature was insufficient to stress-test the workflow |
| TL model | `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` | Test Opus-as-orchestrator theory; verify sub-agent invocation |
| Architect model | `Claude Opus 4.6 (copilot)` | Keep as-is; worked in first run |
| Coder model | `GPT-5.3-Codex (copilot)` | Keep as-is; give it another chance with simplified plan |
| PM model | Haiku | Keep as-is; worked first try |
| Reviewer model | `Gemini 3.1 Pro (Preview) (copilot)` | Keep as-is |
| Fixes to apply before run | F-1 (CRITICAL), F-2 (HIGH), F-3 (MEDIUM), F-4 (LOW) | All are prompt changes, low risk |

### Second Run: Observation Checklist

#### User Notes:
- Started at 16:48 with opus as Lead.
- Opus returned back with clarification questions, which is good sign.
- Only after clarification Opus moved to requirement lock
- TL passed to PM for story creation (model is picked up correctly now - Sonnet)
- First issue spotted - Opus uses significantly more context - 32% till this point and we are at the beginning only.
- PM returned a story which was accepted, TL moved to Architect. And in this set up Architect was selected correctly - as Opus as well. Looks like clear bug (or undocumented behaviour) from github - Opus clearly can be set as a model only if calling model is 3x.
- Plan is created and accepted by TL. The result is passed to Reviewer for Phae 1 checks. Reviewer model is chosen correctly.
- At this point 36% of context is used. 14 mins run.
- Reviewer claimed but didnt create a documentation -> TL returns to Reviewer for correlation (maybe change the model - no reviewers problems when run in CLI)
- TL confirms and moves to Coder (Now the plan has only 2 slices - not sure good or not - plan needs clear verification). Coder is Codex as expected.
- Coder is experiencing errors while running scripts (looks like code related) -38% context used, 20mins run
- Coder passed back to TL, but didn't produce output.
- TL spotted component test failure and passed back to Coder - we definitely need to think if Codex is a right choice.
- 46% context used, 28 mins run
- Coder returns feature to TL, hands-off ok, TL verifes the checks
- 100% context used, should be compacting soon.
- Verification passed, TL prepars to pass to Reviewer for Phase 2.
- Passed to Reviewer
- Context 53% (assumed compaction), 40 mins run
- Finished properly (phase 2 checks passed, no red card) in 40 mins - improvement considering last run in v2.2.0, but rotting context issue appeared again (more realistically beacuse of Opus as lead)

### Quick Code verification

- API works, but I think error code returning for 404 is a bit misleading - I'd prefer 404, ISSUE_NOT_FOUND or ITEM_NOT FOUND, as integration not found refers more to gitlab connectivity issues rather than missing ticket.
- Tests and checks are green
- From first glaance code looks decent, but more in-depth review is needed (Spotted looking like a candidate for refactoing - building a url for gitlab in each method in adapter, we should move it to consolidated place).

I'd like to re-run with equal story with just update of agents - GPT as lead, Sonnet as architect (as Opus can e set up only when lead is Opus) and Sonnet for Coder, for Reviewer let it be Codex or GPT, I don't think Gemini is good for this role.

### Refactoring before third run
- Refactoring implemented for v2.3.0 - key change - URL factory for gitlab - general refactoring, has nothing to do with the second run

### Very important update from the flow
We introduced Karate tool for smoke-tests instead of buggy curl mechanism used before. Flow needs to be adjusted accordingly. 

### Third run (without Opus as lead)
Agents are: TL - GPT-5.4, Architect - Sonnet 4.6, Coder - Sonnet 4.6, PM - Haiku, Reviewer - Codex 5.3

- Run started at 11:20
- TL locked reqs and passed to PM (Haiku now) to proceed with a story. Story was created in less than a minte and passed the TL gate, contdext usage was about 7% which is impressive.
- TL passed req-lock and story to Architect for plan creation (Sonnet was picked up properly). Architect is going through the project structure - potentially it's a bottleneck as application grows. Real architect keeps high-level context in his head and usually doesn't need to scan the code extensively - we need to think how to give kind of "human-like" perspective here. It takes quite long just to search all nessasery fles for Sonnet even now. - Also possible context rotting here in the future. Next iteration should focus more not only on context-map concept, but on agents-ready and always up-to-date context library which should help architect and coder to narrow down their scans to cretian areas.
- For some reasons Architect went to previous plan implementations - it's concerning a bit, as there should be no reason for this and it's confusing fr the model as code was refactored after... - the same idea as above - narrow down focus to contex-map nd context-library.
- In total Architect already investigated few dozens of resources, many of them look completely unrelated to his task. I think we should raise it a top priority for the next version.
- More than 10 mins and Architect has just started to create plan (also he's now supposed to write karate tests, I need to check if it does it and if we have clear gates to verify their existance) - I suspect internal context rotting here, but not proved yet - can be network or load issues as well as it looks kind of frozen at the moment.
- It took 20 mins for Architect to create a plan and karate tests.
- TL accepts the plan and passes to Reviewer phase 1. Reviewer is Codex this time. 
- Time: 25 mins, context 8% (We significantly improved in TL's context, but what happened with Architect is still a bit unclear)
- Codex started to re-invent a wheel in terminal again. - looks like it's really model preference... Now it's waisting signoficant amount of time fighting with termical in order to update review artifact - very simple action should not cause such a struggle. Codex is excellent working solo, but in the orchestration it's one of the weakest models so far..
- Review phase 1 passed and TL moved the task to Coder (it's Sonet this time)
- 29 mins, context: 9%
- Looks like this time architect decided not to split the feature and provided everything as 1 slice... I'm not sure in this decision - looks like Architect's prompt should be modified further - for now e have 2 options - create as many slices as possible or put everything under 1 huge slice... Maybe re-think a concept of slicing at all... And it should be already defined in a propt - he amount of work which is suitable for the models...
- First attempt of Coder failed: TL: The coder handoff failed at the tool level, not the workflow gate. I’m retrying the same approved slice with the checkpoint as the only context source.
- TL returns back to Coder, Coder makes fixes, but then TL: The first coder handoff is invalid on disk: the required implementation report and verification log do not exist, so I’m recording that rejected handoff in the checkpoint and retrying with explicit failure evidence.
- Looks like not a model issue - Sonnet shows the same issues as Codex before...
- 50 mins, 9% of context used. Now looks like time is becoming more concerning issue than context (at least on TL side)
- The second attempt also failed: TL: The second coder attempt hit an upstream capacity error, not a workflow result. I’m retrying once more with the same locked context; if the model provider keeps failing, I’ll have to report that as an external blocker.
- Looks like actual issue with cognitive coder overload was not resolved and Sonnet performs no better than Codex. Also possible reason can be an Architect who didn't split the work properly. But anyway the task initially is not so big to fail it.
- Now looks like we definitely need to have a fourth run as the main issue from the previous version is still not resolved.
- TL re-jects again: TL: The codebase now has a partial implementation on disk, but the handoff is still rejected because the required delivery artifacts are missing and there are known failing expectations to resolve. I’m refreshing the checkpoint with that exact evidence and sending the coder back to complete the batch.
- 1h, 10% context used, Coder is again fixing
- The only positive thing for now - actually qualuty gates are working as for now, at least they do not allow not working code to pass to the user.
- The question now - who is the reason of the problem - Architect or Coder? Or both?
- Coder eventually updated docs for handoff, TL runs independant checks. Quality checks are passed. Started smoke karate testing. It' the first time we try this in run - looks headier then expected, TL started the app and waisiting tim just waiting... I can'see that smoke tests are runnung. And TL is just sitting and waisting time. Tests started about 3 mins later... Consider to simplify the run (enable default mechanism as mvn verify???)
- Check failed: TL: The Team Lead live verification failed, so this cannot advance to Phase 2. The failure is isolated: the new Karate feature uses an invalid Then status != 200 assertion, which makes the smoke suite fail on the non-existent issue scenario. I’m recording this as the first red card and sending the exact evidence back to the coder with a requirement to update the report and verification log.
- 1h 10mins, 25% of contect used.
- Coder is adjusting the code. I'm still returning back to the thought, that coder should have 1 explicit command at the end to run and then it should go - error -fix - rerun until i's green. I see how it writes the code - everything in 1 goal, not like humans who create a piece and checks if it works. Not sure how it's better to implement it. 
- Nice - now Coder if fixing .feature issue - actually it's an Architect who provided bad tests and caused the failure of check.
- Eventually we are moving to Review Phase 2.
- 1h 15mins, 28% context used.
- Just to highlight my goal for such feature size is about 30-40 mins max... A lo of things to fix yet.
- 1h 20 mins - more than 5 mins for review... The same stuck model look - 1 ourput which is not changing for few miins - context or network?
- Reviewer couldn't find proper tools reports - issue with Reviewer most likely as all quality checks were run few times and some reports should be on the disk. Reviewer re-run checks. Again Codex is actually struggling to navigate inside the project (but maybe we are not explicit where to check the report - now it's trying to find evidences for karate reports: ls flow-orchestrator/target | rg 'failsafe|karate')
- Again buch of terminal commands, not sure if there is any sense in Reviewer's actions...
- 1h 30mins, Reviewer is still working, just passed to TL as NOT_PASSED: TL: Two of the three Phase 2 blockers are evidence gaps from the Team Lead runs, and those are within my allowed artifact scope. I’m patching the verification log and the evidence sections of the implementation report now, then I’ll send the remaining documentation contradiction back to the coder.
- The reason of blocking is odd: The artifact evidence gap is now closed on disk. One Phase 2 blocker remains and it is implementation-owned: the context map still claims IssueDto is the single response shape for all issue endpoints, which contradicts the new IssueDetailDto. I’m recording the blocked review and sending that exact doc fix back to the coder.
But realistically it's flow gap - no clear instructions when and who should update their sources of truth.
- TL returned back to coder to fix: context-map!!! At least it should return to Architect, not coder... But it definitely should be handled differenly. Good, that they spotted it, bad the way it's handled and bad that i's flow gap.
- Coder makes the fix, TL passes back to Reviewer.
- 1h 35mins, 32% context used.
- I hope Reviewer have a memory of previous review and is not going to re-run the whole checks... but we will see.. Now I think that actually it's a strong signal that reviewer cought such mismatch... Again reviewer is plaing with the terinal - does it enjoy the process??? He's going through the code again... In this particular case it's redundant - but heavily depends on the character of fixes - if code would be touched it would be helpful to re-run all checkas, but not in this case. He's running all the checks again...
- Again check didn't pass: TL: Phase 2 is now down to a single documentation typo in the context map, and that file is explicitly a Team Lead maintenance artifact. I’m fixing that line directly, then I’ll rerun Reviewer once more.
- We are back to the reviewer again and it will be starting all checks again - that is enourmous waiste of reaources, this scenario should be handled by flow, agents can't manage it on it's own.
- For some reasons Reviewer runs git commands - REJECT explicitely git commands from flow agents -> add it in the flow-rules.
- Eventually the check passed and TL created sign-off
- Total time: 2h (!!!), context used: 35% (not so bad for 2h run)

## App verifications

- API returns proper data as expected, response payload looks good. 
- App starts, no issues or errors.
- Karate passed
- final-check script passed

## Code observations (quick check)

- Can this be covered by some annotation instead? Too trivial check to have it like this which will be needed in all places. Important nore: this pattern was not intricude in this run, it was properly reused in this pattern, but I'm challenging the approach here. It's Issues Controller.
 ```java
private void validateIssueId(final long issueId) {
        if (issueId <= 0) {
            throw new ValidationException(REQUEST_VALIDATION_FAILED, List.of(ISSUE_ID_POSITIVE_MESSAGE));
        }
    }
```
- Is Gitlab Adapter now is a thin wrapper above rest client? If yes - it's ok, but are we explicit about it? I'm challenging name it as adapter... Not sure, smth to think about.

- CRITICAL ISSUE: GitLabIssueDetailMapper lays in integration layer while the story explicitely told to keep this mapping logic only in orchestration!!! IssueDetail in vurrent format belongs to Orchestration. Check where the failure happened. Most likely the work of Architect was not good. -> refactoring for done immidiately after the run 

- I haven't checked tests yet

- Also I'm challenging the approach of records as they need to be created via new and passing bunch of arguments - looks messy and dirty, depends on order, not hande at all. Not introduced in this run but I'd like to know our refactoring options.

### Fixes applied after third run (pre fourth-run)

Based on flow-reviewer analysis of the third run, the following changes were applied:

| # | Fix | File(s) changed | Addresses |
|---|-----|-----------------|-----------|
| F-6 | Added slice upper bound (~8-10 changed files max per slice) to prevent monolithic slices that overload coder | `.github/agents/java-architect.agent.md` | Run 3: Architect produced 1 slice with ~20 files, causing 4+ coder retries |
| F-7 | Changed Reviewer model from Codex 5.3 → Sonnet 4.6. Codex consistently struggled with structured evidence validation and defaulted to terminal exploration | `.github/agents/reviewer.agent.md` | Run 3: Reviewer ran git commands, fought terminal for simple artifact writes, re-ran all checks on doc-only fixes |
| F-8 | Added Phase 1 Reviewer rule: cross-reference every story constraint against `constitution.md`, report blocker on conflict. Added plan prose↔class-table consistency check | `.github/agents/reviewer.agent.md` | Run 3: Story mapping constraint contradicted constitution Principles 2 and 8, not caught by Phase 1 |
| F-9 | Added Phase 1 Reviewer rule: validate structural correctness of Architect-written Karate `.feature` files (syntax, assertions, tags — no execution) | `.github/agents/reviewer.agent.md` | Run 3: Architect wrote invalid `Then status != 200` assertion, causing red card at TL smoke testing |
| F-10 | Added global git ban for ALL flow agents in `delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` § Workflow Control | `.github/instructions/delivery-flow-rules.instructions.md` | Run 3: Reviewer ran git commands despite TL already having a local git ban |
| F-11 | Added PM constitution reference: PM must read `constitution.md` before writing locked constraints; must blocker on conflict | `.github/agents/product-manager.agent.md` | Run 3: PM wrote "mapping must happen in orchestration" constraint that contradicted constitution Principle 8 |

Track these during the run and record results in Run Noes:

| # | What to observe | Why it matters |
|---|-----------------|----------------|
| O-1 | Which model actually executes for each agent | Confirms IDE model assignment works correctly |
| O-2 | How many slices does Architect produce | Validates F-2 (proportional sizing) |
| O-3 | How many coder batches are needed | Validates F-2 + F-3 interaction |
| O-4 | Does TL attempt git operations | Validates F-1 |
| O-5 | Context % at each stage transition | Tracks whether fixes reduce overhead |
| O-6 | Time at each stage transition | Tracks wall-clock improvement |
| O-7 | Does Reviewer produce meaningful code quality findings | Validates the Phase 2 code quality review instructions added in v2.3.0 |
| O-8 | Does TL skip redundant post-Reviewer terminal commands | Validates F-4 |
| O-9 | Coder terminal behavior — mega-commands or clean | Validates the simplified coder evidence instructions from v2.3.0 |
| O-10 | End-to-end: context %, time, premium requests | Compare to first run: 45% / 38min |
